Acetals vs. hemiacetal
Cyclic monosaccharaides contain hemiacetals :
A Carbon that is attached to an OH and OR

Acetals vs. hemiacetal
Cyclic monosaccharaides contain hemiacetals
Identify the hemiacetal Carbon in Glucose:

Acetals vs. hemiacetal
Cyclic monosaccharaides contain hemiacetals
Identify the hemiacetal Carbon in fructose:

Disaccharides
Disaccharides are composed of two monosaccharides.
They link together by forming an acetal :
A carbon that is bonded to 2 OR groups

Disaccharides
When this reaction occurs between two monosaccharides, the bond that joins them together is called a glycosidic linkage .
H 2 O is removed
In this reaction

Disaccharides
Remember the Famous disaccharides:
Maltose (Malt Sugar): Glucose-Glucose
Lactose (Milk Sugar): Glucose- Galactose
Sucrose (Table Sugar): Glucose-Fructose

Disaccharides
The glycosidic linkage joining the two rings can be alpha ( a) or beta ( b) .
If the bond is alpha ( a ), the glysodisidic bonds points down .

Disaccharides
If the bond is beta ( b ), the glysodisidic bonds points up .

Disaccharides
Hydrolysis of maltose yields 2 glucose molecules.
Hydrolysis cleaves the C—O glycosidic linkage and forms two monosaccharides.

Focus on Health & Medicine A. Lactose Intolerance
Lactose is the disaccharide in milk; it consists of 1 galactose ring and 1 glucose ring joined by a 1 4- b -glycosidic bond .

Focus on Health & Medicine A. Lactose Intolerance
The disaccharide bond is cleaved by the enzyme lactase in the body.
Individuals who are lactose intolerant have low levels of this enzyme.
Without the enzyme , lactose cannot be digested , causing abdominal cramps and diarrhea.

Lactase mechanism
Lactase catalysis the hydrolysis of the disaccharide
Initial nucleophilic attack carried out by carboxylic acid side chain, resulting in covalent-enzyme intermediate known as galactosyl -enzyme intermed .
Water breaks the intermediate to release galactose

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Sucrose (table sugar) is a disaccharide consisting of 1 glucose ring and 1 fructose ring .
Sucrose is very sweet, but contains many calories .
To reduce caloric intake, many artificial sweeteners have been developed.
Is the glycosidic bond in sucrose alpha or beta?
Since both anomeric carbons are connected, it’s alpha-1 on the glucose and beta-2 on the fructose

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Aspartame (sold as Equal): Not actually a sugar, but has a very sweet taste

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Saccharine (sold at Sweet’ n Low) was used extensively during World War I.

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Sucralose (sold as Splenda ) has a very similar structure to sucrose.
500X sweeter than sucrose. Cannot be broken down by the body.

Polysaccharides A. Cellulose
Polysaccharides contain three or more monosaccharides joined together.
Cellulose is an unbranched polymer made up of repeating glucose units joined by 1 4- b -glycosidic linkages .
Cellulose is found in the cell walls of all plants, where it gives support and rigidity to wood, plant stems, and grass.
Humans do not posses the enzyme to hydrolyze cellulose ( b -glycosidase) and cannot digest it .

Polysaccharides A. Cellulose
Cellulose makes up the insoluble fiber .

Polysaccharides B. Starch
Starch is a polymer made up of repeating glucose units joined by - glycosodic linkages .
Starch is present in corn , rice , wheat , and potatoes .
The first main type of starch is amylose :

Polysaccharides B. Starch
The second type of starch is amylopectin .

Polysaccharides B. Starch
Amylose is an unbranched polymer linked by 1 4- a -glycosidic linkages .
Amylopectin is a branched polymer linked by 1 4- a and 16- a -glycosidic linkages .
Both starch molecules can be digested by humans using the enzyme amylase .

Polysaccharides C. Glycogen
Glycogen is the major form of polysaccharide storage in animals , similar in structure to amylopectin.
It is stored mainly in the liver and in muscle cells.
When glucose is needed for energy , glucose units are hydrolyzed from the ends of the glycogen polymer.
Because glycogen is highly branched , there are many ends available for hydrolysis.
Polysaccharides C. Glycogen

Polysaccharides C. Glycogen
During the fasting state, which of the following is true?
The liver is converting glucose into glycogen
The muscles are releasing glycogen in the blood
The liver breaks down glycogen to release glucose into the blood
Glycogen in converted into fatty acids

20.6	Polysaccharides C. Glycogen
Fed state: (right after a meal)
Liver takes up glucose from the blood to prevent a spike in glucose level
Glucose is converted into glycogen in the muscle and in the liver for short-term storage
Fasting state: (no food for ~ 6-12 hrs )
Muscle glycogen is used locally by the muscles
Liver glycogen in broken down into glucose, which is released into the blood to maintain normal glucose levels

Focus on the Human Body A. Glycosaminoglycans
Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) are a group of unbranched carbohydrates derived from alternating amino sugar and glucuronate units .
Examples include hyaluronate , extracellular fluids that lubricate joints and in the vitreous humor of the eye.
Amino Sugar
Carboxylate
Glucuronate :
Sugar with a
Carboxylate group

Focus on the Human Body A. Glycosaminoglycans
Another example is chondroitin , a component of cartilage and tendons.

Focus on the Human Body A. Glycosaminoglycans
Heparin , stored in the mast cells of the liver, helps prevent blood clotting. It is an anticoagulant.
Focus on the Human Body A. Glycosaminoglycans

Focus on the Human Body B. Chitin
Chitin is a polysaccharide formed from N -acetyl- D -glucosamine units joined together by 1 4- b -glycosidic linkages .
N-acetyl
Chitin is in the exoskeleton of crustaceans & insects , cell wall of fungi

Reducing sugars
A reducing sugar is a sugar than can reduce another molecule, while in the process becoming oxidized
Only sugars with a free anomeric carbon are reducing sugars
Reducing sugars are sugars with free anomeric carbons - they will reduce oxidizing agents, such as peroxide and some metals (Cu 2+ and Ag + ).
The sugar is oxidized in the process

Glucose is a reducing sugar
Glucose can reduce Cu 2+ to Cu +
The reaction produces red precipitate, can be used to detect the presence of glucose (reducing sugars)

Polysaccharides as reducing sugars
Amylose and cellulose are both reducing polysaccharides
Each contains a “reducing end”: exposed anomeric Carbon

Disaccharides
Not all disaccharides are reducing sugars
Lactose, maltose and cellobiose are all reducing
Sucrose is non-reducing: does not contain a free anomeric carbon

Focus on the Human Body Blood Type
There are four blood types— A , B , AB , and O .
Blood type is based on 3 or 4 monosaccharides attached to a membrane protein of red blood cells.
Each blood type has the monosaccharides below:

Focus on the Human Body Blood Type
Type A blood contains a fourth monosaccharide:
Type B contains an additional D -galactose unit.
Type AB has both type A and type B carbohydrates.
Cell Membranes A. Structure of the Cell Membrane
Focus on the Human Body Blood Type

Focus on the Human Body Blood Type
The blood of one individual may contain antibodies to another type.
Those with type O blood are called universal donors , because people with any other blood type have no antibodies to type O.
Those with type AB blood are universal recipients because their blood contains no antibodies to A, B, or O.

Question
You’re a nurse in a small clinic treating a victim of a car accident who has lost lots of blood. She is unconscious, but her blood donor card says that her blood type is AB. You check your blood reserve and all you have is bags of blood type A, and bags of blood type B. What is the best course of action?
You must mix the A and B blood types before administering to the patient
You can only give your patient blood type AB, so you survey your co-workers for someone with AB blood type
You can only give your patient blood type O, so you survey your co-workers for someone with O blood type
You quickly administer blood type A
You quit your job and join the circus like you had always wanted

Bacterial Cell Walls
Composed of 1 or 2 bilayers and peptidoglycan shell
Gram-positive: One membrane phospholipid bilayer and thick peptidoglycan outer shell
Gram-negative: Two membrane phospholipid bilayers with thin peptidoglycan shell in between
Gram-positive: pentaglycine bridge connects tetrapeptides
Gram-negative: direct amide bond between tetrapeptides

Bacterial cell wall
Peptidoglycan: major component of bacterial cell wall
Made by crosslinking peptides and sugars

The Structure of Peptidoglycan
Figure 7.29  The tetrapeptides linking adjacent backbone chains contain an unusual γ -carboxyl linkage.
Note the isoglutamate linkage in the tetrapeptide chain

The Structure of Gram-positive Peptidoglycan
Figure 7.29 The crosslink between tetrapeptides in Gram-positive cell walls is a pentaglycine bridge.

The Structure of Gram-negative Peptidoglycan
Note the direct crosslinks between tetrapeptide units
Figure 7.29 In Gram-negative cell walls, the linkage between tetrapeptide segments involves a direct amide bond.

The Cell Wall of Gram-positive Bacteria
Figure 7.30  The Gram-positive cell wall consists of a single bilayer membrane with a peptidoglycan coat.  The peptidoglycan layer reacts with Gram stain.

The Cell Wall of Gram-negative Bacteria
Figure 7.30 The cell wall in Gram-negative bacteria consists of two bilayers with peptidoglycan in between

Cell wall synthesis: transpeptidase
Linking two chains of peptidoglycan
Substrate contains D- Ala -D- Ala bond
One alanine is lost in the process of crosslinking

Penicillin and cell wall
Penicillin is a suicide inhibitor of transpeptidase
Penicillin is structurally very similar to the product
Beta lactam ring

Penicillin: Suicide inhibition
Serine in the active site attacks the carbonyl
Result: ring opening, ionic stabilization
Penicillin binding is irreversible, arrests bacterial cell division

Penicillin resistance
Bacteria have developed resistance to penicillin by enzymatic degradation of the beta lactam ring
The enzyme beta-lactamase breaks down the antibiotic before it can inhibit cell wall synthesis
